topics.meaning: Belgium is a small European country with a complex mix of Dutch-speaking, French-speaking and German-speaking communities living in different regions.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Belgium is a small country in Europe, smaller in area than Haryana, sharing borders with France, the Netherlands, Germany and Luxembourg. Its ethnic composition is very complex: 59 per cent of the population lives in the Flemish region and speaks Dutch, another 40 per cent lives in the Wallonia region and speaks French, and the remaining 1 per cent speaks German. In the capital city Brussels, the situation is reversed - 80 per cent of people speak French while 20 per cent are Dutch-speaking. The minority French-speaking community was relatively rich and powerful, which was resented by the Dutch-speaking community who got the benefits of economic development and education much later. This led to tensions between the two communities during the 1950s and 1960s, and the tension was especially acute in Brussels because the Dutch-speaking people were a majority in the country but a minority in the capital.
topics.easyExplanation: Belgium has Dutch, French and German speakers, and the rich French-speaking minority once dominated, which created tension with the Dutch-speaking majority.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Belgium is a small European country with a population of just over one crore but a very mixed population.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The French-speaking minority was richer and more powerful, while the Dutch-speakers got development and education later.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: These differences led to tension between communities in the 1950s and 1960s, most acutely in Brussels.

topics.meaning: Majoritarianism is the belief that the majority community should be able to rule a country in whichever way it wants, disregarding the wishes of the minority.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Sri Lanka emerged as an independent country in 1948, with the major social groups being the Sinhala-speakers (74 per cent) and the Tamil-speakers (18 per cent). The leaders of the Sinhala community sought to secure dominance over government by virtue of their majority, so the democratically elected government adopted a series of majoritarian measures to establish Sinhala supremacy. In 1956, an Act was passed recognising Sinhala as the only official language, thus disregarding Tamil; governments followed preferential policies favouring Sinhala applicants for university positions and government jobs; and a new constitution stipulated that the state shall protect and foster Buddhism. These measures gradually increased the feeling of alienation among the Sri Lankan Tamils, who felt the government denied them equal political rights, discriminated against them in jobs, and ignored their interests. The relations strained over time, the distrust turned into widespread conflict and finally a civil war that caused thousands of deaths and ended only in 2009.
topics.easyExplanation: In Sri Lanka the Sinhala majority forced its will on the Tamil minority through language, job and religion policies, which alienated the Tamils and eventually led to a civil war.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Sri Lanka became independent in 1948 with Sinhala (74%) majority and Tamil (18%) minority.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Sinhala supremacy
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Sinhala leaders used their majority to adopt measures establishing Sinhala dominance over government.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Majoritarian laws

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Strained relations turned into a civil war that killed thousands and ended only in 2009.

The Sri Lankan constitution stipulated that the state shall protect and foster Buddhism, favouring the Sinhala-Buddhist majority.

importantKeywords.definition: A belief that the majority community should be able to rule a country in whichever way it wants, disregarding the wishes and needs of the minority.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Sri Lanka's policy of making only Sinhala the official language is an example of majoritarianism.

importantKeywords.definition: A violent conflict between opposing groups within a country that becomes so intense that it appears like a war.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: The conflict between Sinhalas and Tamils in Sri Lanka turned into a civil war that ended in 2009.

The Sinhala leaders used majoritarian measures - making Sinhala the only official language, favouring Sinhalas in jobs and universities, and protecting Buddhism. These steps alienated the Tamils, strained relations, and led to a destructive civil war that ended in 2009. Sri Lanka shows how refusing to share power can undermine a country's unity.

topics.meaning: Accommodation in Belgium refers to the innovative power-sharing arrangement Belgian leaders worked out to let different communities live together within one country.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Unlike Sri Lanka, Belgian leaders recognised the existence of regional differences and cultural diversities and, between 1970 and 1993, amended their constitution four times to work out an arrangement that would let everyone live together. Under the Belgian model, the constitution prescribes that the number of Dutch and French-speaking ministers shall be equal in the central government, and many powers of the central government were given to the state governments of the two regions, which are not subordinate to the central government. Brussels has a separate government in which both communities have equal representation, with the French-speaking people accepting equal representation there because the Dutch-speaking community accepted equal representation in the central government. There is also a third kind of government, the community government, elected by people belonging to one language community (Dutch, French or German) regardless of where they live, which has power over cultural, educational and language-related issues. Though the Belgian model is complicated, these arrangements worked well, helped avoid civic strife and a possible division of the country, and Brussels was chosen as the headquarters of the European Union.
topics.easyExplanation: Belgium avoided conflict by sharing power equally between communities, giving regions real powers, and creating a special community government for language and culture.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Recognised diversity
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Belgian leaders accepted regional and cultural differences instead of ignoring them.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Equal ministers
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The constitution requires equal numbers of Dutch and French-speaking ministers in the central government.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Strong states
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Many central powers were given to state governments of the two regions, which are not subordinate to the centre.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Brussels balance
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Brussels has its own government with equal representation for both communities.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Community government
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A separate community government handles cultural, educational and language matters for each language group.

examples: The Belgian constitution requires the number of Dutch and French-speaking ministers to be equal in the central government.
Belgium created a community government elected by each language community to control cultural, educational and language issues.

importantKeywords.definition: An arrangement that lets different communities adjust and live together by respecting each other's interests.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Belgium's equal representation rule is a form of accommodation between its communities.

importantKeywords.definition: A government elected by people of one language community to look after cultural, educational and language-related issues, no matter where they live.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Belgium's Dutch community government decides language and education matters for Dutch-speakers.

importantKeywords.definition: A formal change made to the constitution.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Belgium amended its constitution four times between 1970 and 1993 to share power.

topics.quickSummary: Belgian leaders recognised cultural diversity and amended their constitution four times (1970-1993) to share power. They ensured equal Dutch and French-speaking ministers, gave real powers to state governments, balanced representation in Brussels, and created a community government for cultural and language matters. Though complicated, the model avoided civic strife and a split of the country, and Brussels became the EU headquarters.

Equal Dutch and French ministers at the centre.
State governments not subordinate to the centre.

topics.topic: Lessons from Belgium and Sri Lanka
topics.meaning: Comparing Belgium and Sri Lanka teaches that respecting and sharing power with all communities keeps a country united, while imposing majority dominance undermines it.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Both Belgium and Sri Lanka are democracies, yet they dealt with the question of power-sharing very differently. In Belgium, the leaders realised that the unity of the country was possible only by respecting the feelings and interests of different communities and regions, and this realisation resulted in mutually acceptable arrangements for sharing power. Sri Lanka shows a contrasting example - it shows that if a majority community wants to force its dominance over others and refuses to share power, it can undermine the unity of the country. Tyranny of the majority is not just oppressive for the minority; it often affects the majority adversely as well. Imposing the will of the majority community over others may look like an attractive option in the short run, but in the long run it undermines the unity of the nation. Thus the two stories yield the general conclusion that intelligent and willing power-sharing is essential for the stability and unity of any democracy.
topics.easyExplanation: Belgium stayed united by sharing power, while Sri Lanka's majority dominance caused conflict - showing that sharing power keeps a country together.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Both are democracies
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Belgium and Sri Lanka are both democracies but handled power-sharing very differently.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Belgium's approach
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Belgium respected community feelings and worked out mutually acceptable power-sharing arrangements.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Sri Lanka's approach
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Sri Lanka's majority forced dominance and refused to share power, undermining national unity.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Tyranny harms all
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Majority tyranny is oppressive for the minority and often hurts the majority as well.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Conclusion
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Short-run majority dominance undermines national unity in the long run, so power-sharing is essential.

examples: Belgium's mutually acceptable arrangements kept its communities together and avoided partition.
Sri Lanka's refusal to share power with Tamils led to a civil war that damaged the country's unity.

importantKeywords.definition: A situation where the majority community oppresses or dominates the minority by refusing to share power.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Sri Lanka's Sinhala-only policies are an example of the tyranny of the majority.

importantKeywords.definition: The state of a country staying together as one despite internal differences.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Belgium preserved its unity by respecting all its communities.

importantKeywords.definition: The distribution of political power among different groups so that no single group dominates.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Belgium adopted power-sharing while Sri Lanka rejected it.

topics.quickSummary: Belgium and Sri Lanka are both democracies but handled diversity differently. Belgium respected its communities and shared power, preserving unity, while Sri Lanka's majoritarian dominance led to civil war. The lesson is that tyranny of the majority harms everyone and that intelligent, willing power-sharing is essential to keep a democracy stable and united.
revisionNotes: Belgium shared power and stayed united.
Sri Lanka's majority dominance caused civil war.
Majority tyranny harms minority and majority alike.
Power-sharing is essential for national unity.

topics.topic: Why Power-sharing is Desirable (Prudential and Moral Reasons)
topics.meaning: Power-sharing is desirable both for prudential reasons (it brings better outcomes) and moral reasons (it is the very spirit of democracy).
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Two different sets of reasons can be given in favour of power-sharing. Firstly, power-sharing is good because it helps to reduce the possibility of conflict between social groups; since social conflict often leads to violence and political instability, power-sharing is a good way to ensure the stability of political order. Imposing the will of the majority over others may look attractive in the short run, but in the long run it undermines the unity of the nation. There is a second, deeper reason why power-sharing is good for democracies: power-sharing is the very spirit of democracy, because a democratic rule involves sharing power with those who are affected by its exercise and who have to live with its effects. People have a right to be consulted on how they are to be governed, and a legitimate government is one where citizens, through participation, acquire a stake in the system. The first set of reasons is called prudential, stressing that power-sharing brings better outcomes, while the second set is called moral, emphasising that the very act of power-sharing is valuable in itself.
topics.easyExplanation: Power-sharing is good both because it reduces conflict and brings stability (prudential reason) and because it is the true spirit of democracy where people should be consulted (moral reason).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Two sets of reasons
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: There are prudential reasons and moral reasons for power-sharing.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Reduces conflict
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Power-sharing reduces conflict between social groups and prevents violence and instability.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Ensures stability
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: By avoiding majority dominance, power-sharing ensures the stability of the political order.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Spirit of democracy
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Power-sharing is the very spirit of democracy because those affected by decisions should share in making them.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Prudential vs moral
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Prudential reasons stress better outcomes, while moral reasons value the act of sharing power itself.

examples: A prudential reason: power-sharing among India's communities reduces conflict and brings political stability.
A moral reason: in a democracy, people affected by decisions have the right to be consulted and share power.

importantKeywords.definition: Based on prudence or careful calculation of gains and losses, contrasted with purely moral considerations.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Sharing power to avoid conflict and instability is a prudential reason.

importantKeywords.definition: A reason based on values and what is right, here that power-sharing is the very spirit of democracy.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Consulting people on how they are governed is a moral reason for power-sharing.

importantKeywords.definition: A government in which citizens, through participation, acquire a stake in the system.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A government chosen and consulted by its people is a legitimate government.

topics.quickSummary: Power-sharing is desirable for two reasons. The prudential reason is that it reduces conflict between social groups and ensures the stability of the political order, since majority dominance leads to instability in the long run. The moral reason is that power-sharing is the very spirit of democracy, because people affected by decisions have a right to be consulted, making the government legitimate.
revisionNotes: Prudential reason: reduces conflict, ensures stability.
Moral reason: power-sharing is the spirit of democracy.
People have a right to be consulted on governance.

topics.meaning: Khalil's dilemma is the story of Lebanon's power-sharing pact that fixes top government posts for specific religious communities to keep peace after a civil war.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: In the city of Beirut lived a man called Khalil whose father was an Orthodox Christian and mother a Sunni Muslim, reflecting the cosmopolitan mix of Lebanon's capital, where communities lived together yet fought a bitter civil war in which one of Khalil's uncles was killed. At the end of the civil war, Lebanon's leaders agreed to basic rules for power-sharing among different communities: the country's President must belong to the Maronite sect of Catholic Christians, the Prime Minister must be from the Sunni Muslim community, the Deputy Prime Minister must be an Orthodox Christian, and the Speaker must be a Shi'a Muslim. Under this pact, the Christians agreed not to seek French protection and the Muslims agreed not to seek unification with Syria. When the agreement was made, the Christians and Muslims were nearly equal in population, and both sides have continued to respect it even though the Muslims are now in a clear majority. Khalil dislikes this system because under it the top post is out of his reach, and he wishes Lebanon could simply hold open elections; but his elders, who saw the bloodshed, believe the present system is the best guarantee for peace.
topics.easyExplanation: Lebanon keeps peace after its civil war by reserving its top government posts for specific religious communities, even though some people like Khalil feel this limits their chances.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Civil war background

stepByStepExplanation.title: Fixed posts

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Khalil dislikes the system, but elders see it as the best guarantee for peace.

topics.meaning: Horizontal distribution of power means sharing power among different organs of government - the legislature, executive and judiciary - placed at the same level.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: One common form of power-sharing is among the different organs of government, such as the legislature, executive and judiciary - this is called horizontal distribution of power because it allows different organs placed at the same level to exercise different powers. Such a separation ensures that none of the organs can exercise unlimited power, as each organ checks the others, resulting in a balance of power among various institutions. In a democracy, even though ministers and government officials exercise power, they are responsible to the Parliament or State Assemblies. Similarly, although judges are appointed by the executive, they can check the functioning of the executive or the laws made by the legislatures. This arrangement, in which each organ checks the others, is called a system of checks and balances. The idea of power-sharing emerged in opposition to the notion of undivided political power, because in a democracy people are the source of all political power and power should be distributed among as many citizens as possible.
topics.easyExplanation: Power is shared among the legislature, executive and judiciary at the same level, so each checks the others and no organ becomes too powerful.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Three organs
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Power is shared among the legislature, executive and judiciary.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Same level
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: These organs are at the same level, so the sharing is called horizontal distribution.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Limits power
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Separation ensures no single organ can exercise unlimited power.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Each checks others
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Ministers are responsible to Parliament and judges can check the executive and the laws.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Checks and balances
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: This mutual checking is called a system of checks and balances.

examples: Ministers and officials exercise power but are responsible to the Parliament or State Assemblies.
Judges, though appointed by the executive, can check the executive and laws made by the legislature.

importantKeywords.definition: Sharing of power among different organs of government placed at the same level.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Sharing power between the legislature, executive and judiciary is horizontal distribution.

This arrangement of mutual checking is called a system of checks and balances.

topics.meaning: Vertical distribution of power means sharing power among governments at different levels - such as the central, state and local governments.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Power can be shared among governments at different levels - a general government for the entire country and governments at the provincial or regional level. The general government for the entire country is usually called the federal government, which in India we refer to as the central or union government, while the provincial or regional governments are called by different names in different countries, such as state governments in India. This system is not followed in all countries; some have no provincial or state governments. But in countries like India where there are different levels of government, the constitution clearly lays down the powers of different levels of government - this is what was done in Belgium but refused in Sri Lanka, and it is called the federal division of power. The same principle can be extended to levels of government lower than the state, such as the municipality and panchayat, and this sharing involving higher and lower levels of government is called the vertical division of power.
topics.easyExplanation: Power is shared between central, state and local governments at different levels, with the constitution clearly fixing each level's powers.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Different levels
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Power can be shared among a national government and provincial or regional governments.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Names of governments
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: In India the national government is the union government and the regional ones are state governments.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Not everywhere
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Some countries have no provincial governments, but India does.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Constitution fixes powers
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The constitution clearly lays down the powers of different levels, as in Belgium but not in Sri Lanka.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Vertical division
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Extending this down to municipalities and panchayats is the vertical division of power.

examples: In India power is shared between the central (union) government and the state governments.

importantKeywords.definition: Sharing of power among governments at different levels - higher and lower.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Sharing power between the centre, states and panchayats is vertical distribution.

importantKeywords.definition: The general government for the entire country, called the central or union government in India.

importantKeywords.definition: An arrangement where the constitution clearly lays down the powers of different levels of government.

The constitution clearly fixes the powers of each level, as Belgium did but Sri Lanka refused. This federal division can extend down to municipalities and panchayats, forming the vertical division of power.

topics.topic: Power-sharing among Social Groups and Political Parties
topics.meaning: Power can also be shared among different social groups like religious and linguistic communities, and among political parties, pressure groups and movements.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Power may be shared among different social groups, such as religious and linguistic groups; Belgium's community government is a good example, and in some countries there are constitutional and legal arrangements whereby socially weaker sections and women are represented in legislatures and administration. India's system of reserved constituencies in assemblies and Parliament is meant to give space in government and administration to diverse social groups who would otherwise feel alienated, giving minority communities a fair share in power. Power-sharing arrangements can also be seen in the way political parties, pressure groups and movements control or influence those in power; in a democracy citizens must have freedom to choose among various contenders for power, which takes the form of competition among different parties. This competition ensures power does not remain in one hand, and in the long run power is shared among different parties representing different ideologies and social groups. Sometimes this is direct, as when two or more parties form an alliance to contest elections and, if elected, form a coalition government. Interest groups such as traders, businessmen, industrialists, farmers and workers also share governmental power through participation in committees or by influencing decision-making.
topics.easyExplanation: Power is also shared among social groups through reserved seats, and among political parties and pressure groups through elections, coalitions and influence on government.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Among social groups
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Power can be shared among religious and linguistic groups, as in Belgium's community government.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Reserved constituencies
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: India reserves seats so weaker sections and women get representation and a fair share of power.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Among parties
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Competition among political parties ensures power does not stay in one hand.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Coalitions
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Parties may form alliances and, if elected, share power in a coalition government.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Pressure groups
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Interest groups of traders, farmers and workers influence decisions and share governmental power.

examples: India's reserved constituencies give Scheduled Castes, Scheduled Tribes and weaker sections a share in power.

importantKeywords.definition: A government formed when two or more parties join together to share power.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Allied parties winning an election together form a coalition government.

importantKeywords.definition: An organisation of people with common interests that influences government decisions.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: An association of farmers acting as a pressure group can influence agricultural policy.

topics.quickSummary: Power can be shared among social groups, for example through Belgium's community government or India's reserved constituencies for weaker sections and women. It is also shared among political parties through electoral competition and coalition governments, and among pressure groups and interest groups such as traders, farmers and workers who influence decisions. All these forms ensure power does not stay in one hand.

topics.meaning: Federalism is a system of government in which power is divided between a central authority and various constituent units of the country.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Federalism is a system of government in which the power is divided between a central authority and various constituent units of the country. Usually a federation has two levels of government: one is the government for the entire country that is responsible for a few subjects of common national interest, and the others are governments at the level of provinces or states that look after much of the day-to-day administering of their state. Both these levels of government enjoy their power independent of the other. This contrasts with a unitary system, where either there is only one level of government or the sub-units are subordinate to the central government, which can pass orders to the provincial governments. One of the key changes in the Constitution of Belgium was to reduce the power of the central government and give it to the regional governments, so that Belgium shifted from a unitary to a federal form of government, while Sri Lanka continues to be a unitary system where the national government has all the powers. Though only 25 of the world's 193 countries have federal political systems, their citizens make up 40 per cent of the world's population, and most large countries of the world are federations.
topics.easyExplanation: Federalism divides power between a central government and state or provincial governments, each of which has its own independent powers.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Power is divided
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Federalism divides power between a central authority and constituent units like states.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Two levels
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: There is a government for the whole country and governments at the level of states or provinces.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Independent powers
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Both levels enjoy their power independent of the other.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Contrast with unitary
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: In a unitary system, sub-units are subordinate to the central government, which is not so in a federation.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Belgium's shift
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Belgium moved from unitary to federal by giving constitutional powers to its regions.

importantKeywords.simpleExample: India's division of power between the centre and states is federalism.

importantKeywords.definition: A system where there is one level of government or the sub-units are subordinate to the central government.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Sri Lanka has a unitary government where the national government holds all powers.

importantKeywords.definition: The provinces or states that make up a federation.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: The states of India are the constituent units of the Indian federation.

topics.quickSummary: Federalism is a system in which power is divided between a central authority and constituent units like states, with usually two levels of government - a national government for common matters and state governments for day-to-day administration. Both levels have independent powers, unlike a unitary system where sub-units are subordinate. Belgium became federal while Sri Lanka stayed unitary; only 25 countries are federal but they hold 40 per cent of the world's population.
revisionNotes: Federalism: power divided between centre and units.
Two levels of government with independent powers.
Unitary system: sub-units subordinate to the centre.
Belgium became federal; Sri Lanka stayed unitary.

topics.meaning: Federalism has key features such as multiple levels of government, constitutionally guaranteed powers, a written supreme constitution, and an independent judiciary.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Federalism has several distinctive features. There are two or more levels (or tiers) of government, and different tiers govern the same citizens, but each tier has its own jurisdiction in specific matters of legislation, taxation and administration. The jurisdictions of the respective levels or tiers of government are specified in the constitution, so the existence and authority of each tier of government is constitutionally guaranteed. The fundamental provisions of the constitution cannot be unilaterally changed by one level of government, as such changes require the consent of both levels. Courts have the power to interpret the constitution and the powers of different levels of government, and the highest court acts as an umpire if disputes arise between different levels of government in the exercise of their respective powers. Sources of revenue for each level of government are clearly specified to ensure its financial autonomy. The federal system thus has dual objectives - to safeguard and promote the unity of the country, while also accommodating regional diversity. Two aspects are crucial: governments at different levels should agree to rules of partnership, and there must be mutual trust.
topics.easyExplanation: Federalism means multiple levels of government, each with powers fixed by a constitution that cannot be changed by one level alone, with courts settling disputes and each level having its own revenue.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Multiple tiers
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: There are two or more levels of government governing the same citizens.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Own jurisdiction
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Each tier has its own jurisdiction in legislation, taxation and administration.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Constitutionally guaranteed
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The powers of each level are specified and guaranteed in the constitution.

stepByStepExplanation.title: No unilateral change
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Fundamental provisions cannot be changed by one level alone; both levels must agree.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Courts and revenue
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Courts settle disputes between levels, and each level has its own specified revenue sources.

examples: In India, only the Parliament can make laws on Union List subjects while states alone make laws on State List subjects.
Any change to the power-sharing arrangement in India needs a two-thirds majority of Parliament and ratification by half the states.

importantKeywords.definition: The area or subjects over which a government has the authority to act.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Defence falls under the jurisdiction of India's central government.

importantKeywords.definition: The different levels of government in a federation.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: India has three tiers of government - union, state and local.

importantKeywords.definition: The ability of a level of government to raise and spend its own revenue.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: States have financial autonomy through their own specified sources of revenue.

topics.quickSummary: Federalism has key features: two or more tiers of government governing the same citizens, each with its own jurisdiction; powers of each level guaranteed by the constitution; fundamental provisions that cannot be changed by one level alone; courts to settle disputes between levels; and clearly specified revenue sources for financial autonomy. Its dual objectives are to safeguard national unity while accommodating regional diversity, which requires mutual trust and agreed rules.

topics.topic: Coming Together and Holding Together Federations
topics.meaning: Federations can be formed by independent states coming together, or by a large country holding its diverse regions together by dividing power.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: There are two kinds of routes through which federations have been formed. The first route involves independent states coming together on their own to form a bigger unit, so that by pooling sovereignty and retaining identity they can increase their security - this is called a coming together federation, examples being the USA, Switzerland and Australia. In this type of federation, all the constituent states usually have equal power and are strong vis-a-vis the federal government. The second route is where a large country decides to divide its power between the constituent states and the national government - this is called a holding together federation, examples being India, Spain and Belgium. In this second type, the central government tends to be more powerful vis-a-vis the states, and very often different constituent units of the federation have unequal powers, with some units granted special powers. India is a holding together federation, which is why not all states have identical powers and some, like Jammu and Kashmir, Assam and other northeastern states, enjoy a special status.
topics.easyExplanation: Some federations like the USA form when separate states come together as equals, while others like India form when a big country divides power to hold its regions together.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Two routes
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Federations form either by coming together or by holding together.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Coming together
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Independent states pool sovereignty to form a bigger unit, as in the USA, Switzerland and Australia.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Equal states
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: In coming-together federations, states usually have equal power and are strong against the centre.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Holding together
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A large country divides its power among states, as in India, Spain and Belgium.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Powerful centre
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: In holding-together federations the centre is more powerful and units may have unequal powers.

examples: The USA, Switzerland and Australia are coming together federations of independent states.
India, Spain and Belgium are holding together federations where a large country divides its power.

importantKeywords.definition: A federation formed when independent states join to form a bigger unit with equal power.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: The USA is a coming together federation.

importantKeywords.definition: A federation formed when a large country divides power between the centre and its states.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: India is a holding together federation.

importantKeywords.definition: Extra powers granted to certain states because of their peculiar circumstances.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Some northeastern states of India enjoy a special status under the Constitution.

topics.quickSummary: Federations form by two routes. In a coming together federation (USA, Switzerland, Australia), independent states pool their sovereignty and have equal power, strong against the centre. In a holding together federation (India, Spain, Belgium), a large country divides power among its states; here the centre is more powerful and units may have unequal powers, with some, like some northeastern Indian states, granted special status.

topics.topic: Division of Powers: Union, State and Concurrent Lists
topics.meaning: The Indian Constitution divides subjects into the Union List, State List and Concurrent List to clearly assign powers to the central and state governments.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The Indian Constitution clearly provides a three-fold distribution of legislative powers between the Union Government and the State Governments through three lists. The Union List includes subjects of national importance such as defence, foreign affairs, banking, communications and currency, on which only the Union Government can make laws because we need a uniform policy across the country. The State List contains subjects of state and local importance such as police, trade, commerce, agriculture and irrigation, on which the State Governments alone can make laws. The Concurrent List includes subjects of common interest to both, such as education, forest, trade unions, marriage, adoption and succession, on which both can make laws, but if their laws conflict, the law made by the Union Government prevails. Subjects that do not fall in any of the three lists, or new subjects like computer software that came up after the constitution was made, are called residuary subjects, and the Union Government has the power to legislate on them. There are also Union Territories like Chandigarh, Lakshadweep and Delhi which do not have the powers of a state, and the central government has special powers in running these areas.
topics.easyExplanation: Subjects are split into three lists - the Union List for the centre, the State List for states, and the Concurrent List for both, with the centre's law prevailing in case of conflict.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Union List
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: National subjects like defence and foreign affairs, on which only the centre can make laws.

stepByStepExplanation.title: State List
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: State and local subjects like police and agriculture, on which only states can make laws.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Concurrent List
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Common subjects like education and marriage, on which both centre and states can make laws.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Conflict rule
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: If Union and State laws on a Concurrent subject conflict, the Union law prevails.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Residuary subjects
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: New or unlisted subjects like computer software are residuary, and the Union legislates on them.

examples: Defence and foreign affairs are in the Union List, so only the central government can make laws on them.
Education is in the Concurrent List, so both the centre and states can make laws on it, but the Union law prevails in case of conflict.

importantKeywords.definition: The list of subjects of national importance on which only the Union Government can make laws.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Defence is on the Union List.

importantKeywords.definition: The list of subjects on which both the Union and State Governments can make laws.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Education is on the Concurrent List.

importantKeywords.definition: Subjects not mentioned in any of the three lists, on which the Union Government legislates.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Computer software is a residuary subject handled by the Union Government.

topics.quickSummary: The Indian Constitution divides legislative powers through three lists. The Union List covers national subjects like defence and currency, on which only the centre legislates. The State List covers local subjects like police and agriculture, for states alone. The Concurrent List covers common subjects like education, on which both can legislate, with the Union law prevailing in conflicts. Unlisted or new subjects are residuary and handled by the Union Government, while Union Territories are run with special central powers.

topics.meaning: Linguistic states are states created so that people who speak the same language live in the same state, which strengthened Indian federalism.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The creation of linguistic states was the first and a major test for democratic politics in India. After 1947, the boundaries of several old states were changed to create new states so that people who spoke the same language lived in the same state, while some states such as Nagaland, Uttarakhand and Jharkhand were created to recognise differences based on culture, ethnicity or geography rather than language. When the demand for states on the basis of language was first raised, some national leaders feared it would lead to the disintegration of the country, and the central government resisted linguistic states for some time. But experience has shown that the formation of linguistic states actually made the country more united and made administration easier. If you look at the political map of India in 1947 and that of 2019, you will be surprised by the extent of the changes - many old states have vanished, many new ones have been created, and the areas, boundaries and names of states have changed.
topics.easyExplanation: India reorganised states so that people speaking the same language live together; though leaders first feared it would break the country, it actually made India more united.

stepByStepExplanation.title: First major test
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Creating linguistic states was the first big test of Indian democratic politics.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Same language together
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Boundaries were redrawn so people speaking the same language lived in the same state.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Other bases too
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Some states like Nagaland, Uttarakhand and Jharkhand were based on culture, ethnicity or geography.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Initial fear
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Leaders first feared linguistic states would disintegrate the country and resisted them.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Made India united
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Experience showed linguistic states made the country more united and easier to administer.

Nagaland, Uttarakhand and Jharkhand were created on the basis of culture, ethnicity or geography rather than language.

importantKeywords.simpleExample: India reorganised its states largely on a linguistic basis after independence.

importantKeywords.simpleExample: The reorganisation of states in India was based mostly on language.

topics.quickSummary: The creation of linguistic states was the first major test of Indian democratic politics. State boundaries were redrawn so people speaking the same language lived together, while some states like Nagaland and Jharkhand were based on culture or geography. Although leaders initially feared this would disintegrate the country, experience proved that linguistic states actually made India more united and administration easier.

topics.meaning: India's language policy gave no single language the status of national language and adopted a cautious, flexible approach to promoting Hindi alongside other languages.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A second test for Indian federalism is the language policy. The Constitution did not give the status of national language to any one language; Hindi was identified as the official language, but Hindi is the mother tongue of only about 40 per cent of Indians, so there were many safeguards to protect other languages. Besides Hindi, there are 21 other languages (22 in total in the Eighth Schedule) recognised as Scheduled Languages, and a candidate in a central government examination may take it in any of these languages, while states have their own official languages. According to the Constitution, the use of English for official purposes was to stop in 1965, but many non-Hindi speaking states demanded its continuation, and in Tamil Nadu this movement took a violent form, so the central government agreed to continue the use of English along with Hindi. Promotion of Hindi continues to be the official policy, but promotion does not mean the centre can impose Hindi on states where people speak a different language. Unlike Sri Lanka, the flexibility shown by Indian leaders helped avoid the kind of language conflict that Sri Lanka faced.
topics.easyExplanation: India did not make any one language the national language; it kept Hindi as official but protected many other languages and flexibly continued English to avoid conflict.

stepByStepExplanation.title: No national language
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The Constitution gave no single language the status of national language.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Hindi as official
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Hindi was made the official language but is the mother tongue of only about 40 per cent of Indians.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Scheduled Languages
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Besides Hindi, 21 other languages are recognised as Scheduled Languages with safeguards.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Continuation of English
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: English was to stop in 1965, but states demanded it continue, so it was kept alongside Hindi.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Flexible promotion
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Hindi is promoted but cannot be imposed, and this flexibility avoided Sri Lanka-style conflict.

examples: A candidate in a central government examination may take it in any of the recognised Scheduled Languages, not just Hindi.
Unlike Sri Lanka's Sinhala-only policy, India continued English alongside Hindi after demands from non-Hindi states.

importantKeywords.definition: A language used for the official work of the government, which in India is Hindi at the central level.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Hindi is the official language of the Government of India.

importantKeywords.definition: Languages listed in the Eighth Schedule of the Constitution and given official recognition.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Tamil and Bengali are among India's Scheduled Languages.

importantKeywords.definition: The set of government rules and approach toward the use and promotion of languages.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: India's flexible language policy continued English alongside Hindi.

topics.quickSummary: India's language policy did not give national-language status to any one language. Hindi was made the official language but, being the mother tongue of only about 40 per cent of Indians, was accompanied by safeguards for 21 other Scheduled Languages. Although English was to stop in 1965, demands from non-Hindi states led to its continuation alongside Hindi. This cautious and flexible policy, which does not impose Hindi, helped India avoid the language conflict that troubled Sri Lanka.

Hindi official, but only ~40% mother tongue.
22 Scheduled Languages in the Eighth Schedule.

topics.meaning: Centre-State relations are how power-sharing between the central and state governments actually works, which has been strengthened by the rise of coalition governments.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Restructuring Centre-State relations is one more way in which federalism has been strengthened in practice, because how constitutional arrangements work in reality depends on how ruling parties and leaders follow them. For a long time the same party ruled both at the Centre and in most of the states, which meant state governments did not exercise their rights as autonomous federal units; and when a rival party ruled a state, the central government often misused the Constitution to dismiss those state governments, undermining the spirit of federalism. All this changed significantly after 1990, which saw the rise of regional political parties in many states and the beginning of the era of coalition governments at the Centre. Since no single party got a clear majority in the Lok Sabha, the major national parties had to enter into alliances with many regional parties to form a government, leading to a new culture of power-sharing and respect for the autonomy of state governments. This trend was supported by a major Supreme Court judgement that made it difficult for the central government to dismiss state governments in an arbitrary manner, so federal power-sharing is more effective today than in the early years after the Constitution came into force.
topics.easyExplanation: Earlier the centre often dominated and dismissed rival state governments, but after 1990 coalition governments and a Supreme Court ruling made federalism work better.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Depends on practice
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: How federalism works depends on how ruling parties and leaders follow constitutional arrangements.

stepByStepExplanation.title: One-party dominance
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: For long the same party ruled the centre and most states, weakening state autonomy.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Misuse of power
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The centre often misused the Constitution to dismiss rival state governments.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Change after 1990
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Regional parties rose and coalition governments began, forcing the centre to respect state autonomy.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Supreme Court support
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A Supreme Court judgement made arbitrary dismissal of state governments difficult.

examples: After 1990, national parties had to form alliances with regional parties to govern, respecting state autonomy.
A major Supreme Court judgement made it difficult for the centre to dismiss state governments arbitrarily.

importantKeywords.definition: The relationship and division of power between the central and state governments.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Coalition governments improved Centre-State relations after 1990.

importantKeywords.definition: A government formed by the coming together of at least two political parties.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: After 1990, the Centre was often run by a coalition government of national and regional parties.

importantKeywords.definition: The power of a state government to act independently within its own jurisdiction.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Regional parties demanded greater autonomy for their state governments.

topics.quickSummary: Centre-State relations show how federal power-sharing actually works. Earlier, when one party ruled both the centre and states, state autonomy was weak and the centre often dismissed rival state governments. After 1990, the rise of regional parties and coalition governments forced national parties to respect state autonomy, and a Supreme Court judgement made arbitrary dismissals difficult. As a result, federal power-sharing is far more effective today.
revisionNotes: Federalism in practice depends on ruling parties.
Earlier centre dismissed rival state governments.
After 1990: regional parties and coalitions.
Supreme Court curbed arbitrary dismissals.

topics.meaning: Decentralisation is taking power away from central and state governments and giving it to local governments so that problems can be settled at the local level.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A vast country like India cannot be run only through two tiers, so federal power-sharing in India needs another tier of government below the state level, which is the rationale for decentralisation. When power is taken away from the central and state governments and given to local government, it is called decentralisation, the basic idea being that many problems are best settled at the local level because people have better knowledge of and ideas about their localities, and it allows people to directly participate in decision-making, realising the principle of local self-government. Before 1992, local governments existed but were under the control of state governments, elections were not held regularly, and they had no powers or resources of their own, so there was very little real decentralisation. A major step came in 1992 when the Constitution was amended: it became constitutionally mandatory to hold regular elections to local bodies; seats were reserved for Scheduled Castes, Scheduled Tribes and Other Backward Classes; at least one-third of all positions were reserved for women; an independent State Election Commission was created in each state; and state governments were required to share some powers and revenue with local bodies. Rural local government is known as panchayati raj.
topics.easyExplanation: Decentralisation gives power to local governments like panchayats and municipalities; the 1992 amendment made regular elections, reservations and a State Election Commission mandatory.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Need for a third tier
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A huge country like India needs a level of government below the states.

stepByStepExplanation.title: What decentralisation is
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Giving power from central and state governments to local governments is decentralisation.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Local knowledge
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Local problems are best settled locally because people know their own localities best.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Weak before 1992
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Earlier local bodies had no regular elections, powers or resources of their own.

stepByStepExplanation.title: 1992 amendment
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Regular elections, reservations for SC/ST/OBC and women, and State Election Commissions were made mandatory.

examples: After the 1992 amendment, at least one-third of all seats in local bodies are reserved for women.
Each state set up an independent State Election Commission to conduct panchayat and municipal elections.

importantKeywords.definition: Taking power away from central and state governments and giving it to local government.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Giving powers to panchayats is an example of decentralisation.

importantKeywords.definition: The system of rural local self-government in India.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A gram panchayat is the basic unit of panchayati raj.

importantKeywords.definition: The body of all voters of a village that supervises the gram panchayat.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: The gram sabha approves the annual budget of the gram panchayat.

topics.quickSummary: Decentralisation means giving power from the central and state governments to local governments, because many problems are best settled locally where people have direct knowledge and can participate. Before 1992 local bodies were weak, with no regular elections, powers or resources. The 1992 constitutional amendment made regular elections mandatory, reserved seats for SC, ST, OBC and women (at least one-third), created State Election Commissions, and required states to share powers and revenue.

topics.topic: Structure of Local Government: Panchayati Raj and Municipalities
topics.meaning: Local government in India is organised as panchayati raj in rural areas and municipalities and corporations in urban areas, going up to the district level.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Rural local government, known as panchayati raj, begins with the gram panchayat - a council for a village or group of villages consisting of several ward members (often called panch) and a president or sarpanch, directly elected by all the adult population of the ward or village, and working under the supervision of the gram sabha, which includes all the voters of the village. A few gram panchayats are grouped together to form a panchayat samiti, block or mandal, whose members are elected by the panchayat members of that area, and all the panchayat samitis in a district together constitute the zilla parishad, headed by its chairperson, with members of the Lok Sabha and MLAs of the district also being members. For urban areas, municipalities are set up in towns and municipal corporations in big cities, both controlled by elected bodies; the political head of a municipality is the municipal chairperson and of a corporation the mayor. This new system of local government is the largest experiment in democracy in the world, with about 36 lakh elected representatives, and it has helped deepen democracy and increase women's representation, though gram sabhas are not held regularly and many powers and resources have still not been transferred.
topics.easyExplanation: Rural areas have gram panchayats, panchayat samitis and zilla parishads, while towns have municipalities and big cities have corporations led by a mayor.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Gram panchayat
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The basic rural unit is the gram panchayat with ward members and a sarpanch, supervised by the gram sabha.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Panchayat samiti
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Several gram panchayats form a panchayat samiti, block or mandal.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Zilla parishad
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: All panchayat samitis in a district form the zilla parishad, headed by its chairperson.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Urban bodies
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Towns have municipalities and big cities have municipal corporations led by a mayor.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Largest experiment
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: With about 36 lakh elected representatives, it is the world's largest experiment in democracy.

examples: A village has a gram panchayat headed by a sarpanch, supervised by the gram sabha of all village voters.
Big cities are governed by municipal corporations whose political head is the mayor.

importantKeywords.definition: The council of a village or group of villages, with ward members and a sarpanch.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: The gram panchayat is the decision-making body for the entire village.

importantKeywords.definition: The district-level body formed by all the panchayat samitis in a district.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: The zilla parishad chairperson is the political head of the district's panchayat system.

importantKeywords.definition: The local government body for a big city, headed by a mayor.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Mumbai is governed by a municipal corporation led by a mayor.

topics.quickSummary: India's local government has rural and urban structures. Rural panchayati raj has the gram panchayat (with sarpanch and gram sabha), the panchayat samiti at the block level, and the zilla parishad at the district level. Urban areas have municipalities in towns and municipal corporations led by mayors in big cities. With about 36 lakh elected representatives, this is the world's largest experiment in democracy, though gram sabhas and resource-sharing remain weak.

topics.topic: Gender Division and Sexual Division of Labour
topics.meaning: Gender division is a hierarchical social division between men and women based not on biology but on social expectations and stereotypes.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Gender division is a form of hierarchical social division seen everywhere but rarely recognised in the study of politics. It tends to be understood as natural and unchangeable, but it is not based on biology; rather it is based on social expectations and stereotypes. This is reflected in the sexual division of labour in most families, where women do all the work inside the home such as cooking, cleaning, washing and looking after children, while men do the work outside. It is not that men cannot do housework - when these jobs are paid for, men readily take them up, as most cooks and tailors in hotels are men; nor is it that women do not work outside, since in villages women fetch water, collect fuel and work in the fields, and in urban areas poor women work as domestic helpers while middle-class women work in offices. The result is that although women are half of humanity, their role in public life, especially politics, is minimal in most societies. Earlier, only men were allowed to vote and contest elections, but gradually women in different parts of the world organised and agitated for equal rights through what are called feminist movements.
topics.easyExplanation: Gender division divides work between men and women based on social ideas, not biology, which keeps women's role in public life and politics low in most societies.

stepByStepExplanation.title: A social division
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Gender division is a hierarchical social division seen everywhere.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Not based on biology

stepByStepExplanation.title: Sexual division of labour

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Women's work is often unpaid and not valued, even when they also work outside.

topics.quickSummary: Gender division is a hierarchical social division between men and women based on social expectations and stereotypes, not biology. It shows in the sexual division of labour, where women do unpaid household work and men work outside. As a result women's role in public life and politics stays minimal in most societies. Women organised feminist movements to demand equal political and legal rights.

topics.meaning: Women in India still face inequality in education, wages and safety, and their political representation in legislatures remains very low.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: In India, women still lag much behind men despite some improvement since Independence, as ours is still a male-dominated, patriarchal society. The literacy rate among women is only 54 per cent compared with 76 per cent among men, fewer girls go for higher studies, and the proportion of women in highly paid jobs is small. Although the Equal Remuneration Act of 1976 provides for equal wages for equal work, in almost all areas women are paid less than men even for the same work. Sex-selective abortion has led to a decline in the child sex ratio to 919 in some places, and there are reports of harassment, exploitation and domestic violence against women. Because women's issues are not given adequate attention, many feminists conclude that unless women control power their problems will not be addressed, so one way is to have more women as elected representatives. The percentage of elected women in the Lok Sabha touched 14.36 per cent for the first time in 2019, and their share in state assemblies is less than 5 per cent. Panchayati Raj reserves one-third of seats for women, giving over 10 lakh elected women representatives, and the Nari Shakti Vandan Adhiniyam (Women's Reservation Act, 2023) provides 33 per cent reservation for women in the Lok Sabha and state assemblies.
topics.easyExplanation: Women in India still face lower literacy, unequal wages and unsafety, and their numbers in legislatures are very low, which is why reservations like one-third seats in panchayats and the 2023 Women's Reservation Act matter.

topics.meaning: Religion can enter politics in legitimate ways when it raises moral values and demands equal treatment, but it becomes dangerous when used to dominate other religions.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Religious differences are often expressed in the field of politics, and this is not always wrong or dangerous. Gandhiji used to say that religion can never be separated from politics, by which he meant not any particular religion but the moral values that inform all religions, believing that politics must be guided by ethics drawn from religion. Human rights groups in India have demanded that the government take special steps to prevent communal riots and protect religious minorities, and the women's movement has argued that family laws of all religions discriminate against women and should be made more equitable. All these instances involve a relationship between religion and politics, but they do not seem very wrong because ideas, ideals and values drawn from different religions can and perhaps should play a role in politics. People should be able to express their needs and demands as members of a religious community, and those in power should sometimes regulate the practice of religion to prevent discrimination and oppression. These political acts are not wrong as long as they treat every religion equally - the problem begins only when religion is seen as the basis of the nation.
topics.easyExplanation: Religion can rightly influence politics through moral values and demands for equal treatment, and it is wrong only when it is used to make one religion dominate others.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Religious differences are often expressed in politics, which is not always wrong.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Human rights groups demand protection of minorities and prevention of communal riots.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Limit
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: These acts are acceptable as long as every religion is treated equally.

importantKeywords.simpleExample: Different family laws apply to followers of different religions in India.

People may express demands as members of a religious community, and the state may regulate religion to prevent discrimination. topics.meaning: Communalism is the belief that people belonging to the same religion form one community whose interests are opposed to those of other religious communities.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The problem begins when religion is seen as the basis of the nation, becoming more acute when religion is expressed in politics in exclusive and partisan terms - when one religion is pitted against another and state power is used to establish the domination of one religious group. This is communal politics, based on the idea that religion is the principal basis of social community, that followers of a particular religion must belong to one community with the same fundamental interests, and that people of different religions cannot belong to the same social community. In its extreme form, communalism leads to the belief that people of different religions cannot live as equal citizens within one nation. This belief is fundamentally flawed because people of one religion do not have the same interests in every context, and there are many voices inside every community. Communalism can take various forms: everyday beliefs involving religious prejudices and stereotypes; a quest for political dominance of one's own religious community (majoritarian dominance for the majority, or a desire to form a separate political unit for the minority); political mobilisation on religious lines using sacred symbols and emotional appeals; and in its ugliest form, communal violence, riots and massacre, as India and Pakistan suffered at the time of Partition.
topics.easyExplanation: Communalism is the wrong belief that all followers of one religion form one community opposed to others, which can lead to discrimination, religious mobilisation and even violent riots.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Religion as nation's basis
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The problem begins when religion is treated as the basis of the nation.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Definition
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Communalism is the belief that religion is the principal basis of social community.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Flawed belief
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: It wrongly assumes all followers of one religion share the same interests, ignoring many voices.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Forms
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: It appears as everyday prejudice, quest for dominance, and political mobilisation on religious lines.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Worst form
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: In its ugliest form it leads to communal violence, riots and massacre, as during Partition.

examples: India and Pakistan suffered some of the worst communal riots at the time of Partition.
Political mobilisation using sacred symbols and emotional appeal to bring one religion's followers together is a form of communalism.

importantKeywords.definition: The belief that religion is the principal basis of social community and that the interests of different religious communities are opposed.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Saying people of two religions cannot be equal citizens of one nation is communalism.

importantKeywords.definition: Politics that pits one religion against another and uses state power to dominate one religious group.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Appealing only to voters of one religion against others is communal politics.

importantKeywords.definition: The form communalism takes when the majority religious community seeks to dominate others.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A majority community demanding control over the whole nation shows majoritarian dominance.

topics.quickSummary: Communalism is the belief that religion is the principal basis of social community and that the interests of different religious communities are necessarily opposed. This flawed belief ignores the many voices within each community. It takes forms such as everyday prejudice, a quest for political dominance, political mobilisation on religious lines, and, in its ugliest form, communal violence and riots like those at Partition. It remains a major challenge to Indian democracy.

Flawed - one religion's people don't share all interests.
Forms: prejudice, dominance, mobilisation, violence.
Worst riots occurred during Partition.

topics.meaning: A secular state is one that has no official religion, treats all religions equally, and is one of the foundations of the Indian Constitution.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Communalism was and continues to be one of the major challenges to democracy in India, and the makers of the Constitution, aware of this challenge, chose the model of a secular state, reflected in several constitutional provisions. There is no official religion for the Indian state - unlike the status of Buddhism in Sri Lanka, Islam in Pakistan and Christianity in England, the Constitution does not give a special status to any religion. The Constitution provides all individuals and communities the freedom to profess, practice and propagate any religion, or not to follow any. The Constitution prohibits discrimination on grounds of religion, and at the same time allows the state to intervene in matters of religion to ensure equality within religious communities - for example, it bans untouchability. Understood in this sense, secularism is not just an ideology of some parties but constitutes one of the foundations of the country, because communalism threatens the very idea of India. A secular Constitution like ours is necessary but not sufficient to combat communalism - communal prejudices need to be countered in everyday life and religion-based mobilisation in the arena of politics.
topics.easyExplanation: India is a secular state with no official religion, giving everyone freedom of religion and banning discrimination, which is a basic foundation of the Constitution.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Response to communalism
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Constitution-makers chose a secular state to combat the challenge of communalism.

stepByStepExplanation.title: No official religion
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: India has no official religion, unlike Sri Lanka, Pakistan or England.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Freedom of religion
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Everyone is free to profess, practice and propagate any religion, or none.

stepByStepExplanation.title: No discrimination
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The Constitution prohibits discrimination on grounds of religion and bans untouchability.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Necessary but not sufficient
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A secular Constitution alone cannot defeat communalism; prejudice must be fought in daily life and politics.

topics.quickSummary: To combat communalism, India's Constitution-makers chose a secular state. India has no official religion, unlike Sri Lanka, Pakistan or England; all individuals are free to profess, practice and propagate any religion or none; discrimination on religious grounds is banned; and the state may intervene to ensure equality, as in banning untouchability. Secularism is a basic foundation of the Constitution, though a secular Constitution alone is necessary but not sufficient to defeat communalism.

topics.meaning: The caste system is a social division special to India, based on hereditary occupation sanctioned by rituals and the exclusion of outcaste groups.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Unlike gender and religion, caste division is special to India. All societies have some social inequality and division of labour, and in most societies occupations are passed from one generation to another, but the caste system is an extreme form of this where hereditary occupational division was sanctioned by rituals. Members of the same caste group were supposed to form a social community that practiced the same or similar occupation, married within the caste group and did not eat with members of other caste groups. The caste system was based on the exclusion of and discrimination against the outcaste groups, who were subjected to the inhuman practice of untouchability. Political leaders and social reformers like Jotiba Phule, Gandhiji, B.R. Ambedkar and Periyar Ramaswami Naicker advocated and worked to establish a society in which caste inequalities are absent. Partly due to their efforts and partly due to socio-economic changes such as economic development, large-scale urbanisation, growth of literacy and education, occupational mobility and the weakening of landlords' position in villages, the old notions of caste hierarchy are breaking down. Yet caste has not disappeared, and it continues to be closely linked to economic status, with upper castes still best off and Dalits and Adivasis worst off.
topics.easyExplanation: The caste system, unique to India, divides people by hereditary occupation and once excluded outcaste groups through untouchability; reformers fought it and it is weakening, though caste still shapes economic status.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Special to India
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Caste division is a social division special to India.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Hereditary occupation
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Caste is an extreme form where hereditary occupation was sanctioned by rituals.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Exclusion
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: It was based on exclusion of and discrimination against outcaste groups through untouchability.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Reformers
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Reformers like Phule, Gandhiji, Ambedkar and Periyar worked against caste inequalities.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Weakening but present
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Urbanisation and education weakened caste hierarchy, but it still links to economic status.

Urbanisation, education and occupational mobility have weakened the old notions of caste hierarchy in modern India.

importantKeywords.simpleExample: Marrying only within one's own caste reflects the caste system.

importantKeywords.definition: The inhuman practice of excluding and discriminating against outcaste groups.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: The Constitution bans untouchability.

importantKeywords.simpleExample: Occupational mobility has weakened caste-based jobs in modern India.

topics.quickSummary: The caste system, special to India, is an extreme form of social division in which hereditary occupation was sanctioned by rituals, with people marrying within their caste and excluding outcaste groups through untouchability. Reformers like Phule, Gandhiji, Ambedkar and Periyar fought caste inequalities, and urbanisation, education and occupational mobility have weakened the hierarchy.
