topics.meaning: Caste influences politics through candidate selection, appeals to caste sentiment and vote banks, but no single caste can win elections on its own.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Casteism is rooted in the belief that caste is the sole basis of social community, but this belief is not borne out by experience because caste is only one aspect of our lives. Caste can take various forms in politics: when parties choose candidates, they keep in mind the caste composition of the electorate and nominate candidates from different castes to win; when governments are formed, parties take care that representatives of different castes and tribes find a place; political parties and candidates make appeals to caste sentiment to gather support, and some parties are seen as representatives of certain castes; and universal adult franchise gave new consciousness to castes once treated as inferior. However, the focus on caste can create a false impression that elections are all about caste. In reality, no parliamentary constituency has a clear majority of one single caste, so every candidate must win the confidence of more than one caste and community; no party wins all the votes of a caste; and when people call a caste a vote bank of a party, it only means a large proportion vote for that party. Voters' attachment to parties, their economic condition, and their assessment of the government's performance often matter more than caste, so while caste matters in elections, many other factors do too.
topics.easyExplanation: Caste shapes politics through candidate choices and appeals to caste feeling, but since no constituency has one caste in the majority, parties must win many castes, and other factors also decide elections.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Casteism's belief
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Casteism wrongly assumes caste is the sole basis of social community.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Candidate selection
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Parties nominate candidates keeping the caste composition of the electorate in mind.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Appeals and vote banks
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Parties appeal to caste sentiment, and a caste seen as a vote bank largely votes for one party.

stepByStepExplanation.title: No single-caste majority
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: No constituency has one caste as a clear majority, so parties must win many castes.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Other factors
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Economic condition, party attachment and government performance also decide elections.

examples: When choosing candidates, parties keep in mind the caste composition of the constituency to win support.
No parliamentary constituency has a clear majority of one single caste, so every party must win more than one caste's confidence.

importantKeywords.definition: The belief that caste is the sole basis of social community and that all members of a caste have the same interests.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Assuming everyone of a caste votes the same way is casteism.

importantKeywords.definition: A group, often a caste or community, a large proportion of whom regularly vote for one party.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A caste called a party's vote bank usually gives it most of its votes.

importantKeywords.definition: The right of all adults to vote regardless of caste or community.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Universal adult franchise gave a new consciousness to castes once treated as inferior.

topics.quickSummary: Caste influences politics in several ways: parties choose candidates based on the caste composition of the electorate, ensure different castes are represented in governments, and appeal to caste sentiment, with some castes seen as vote banks. But no constituency has a single-caste majority, no party wins all of a caste's votes, and factors like economic condition, party loyalty and government performance also decide elections. So caste matters, but it is not the only factor.
revisionNotes: Casteism wrongly treats caste as the sole community basis.
Parties choose candidates by caste composition.
Vote bank means most of a caste votes one party.
No constituency has a single-caste majority.

topics.topic: Politics in Caste and Caste Inequality Today
topics.meaning: Just as caste shapes politics, politics also shapes caste by politicising caste identities, while caste continues to be a source of economic inequality.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: There is not only a one-way relation between caste and politics; politics too influences the caste system and caste identities by bringing them into the political arena, so it is the caste that gets politicised. Each caste group tries to become bigger by incorporating neighbouring castes that were earlier excluded, and caste politics has helped people from Dalit and OBC castes to gain better access to decision making. At the same time, caste remains an important source of economic inequality because it regulates access to resources; in the past untouchable castes were denied the right to own land while twice born castes had the right to education, and although such formalised inequality is now outlawed, the effects of centuries of accumulated advantages and disadvantages continue to be felt. Evidence from the National Sample Survey shows that the average economic status of caste groups still follows the old hierarchy - the upper castes are best off, Dalits and Adivasis are worst off, and backward classes are in between; the proportion living in extreme poverty is much higher for the lowest castes; and the upper castes are heavily over-represented among the rich. Thus, while every caste now has some rich and some poor members, caste continues to be strongly linked to economic status.
topics.easyExplanation: Politics also changes caste by politicising it and helping Dalits and OBCs gain power, while caste still shapes economic inequality, with upper castes best off and Dalits and Adivasis worst off.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Two-way relation
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Politics influences caste just as caste influences politics, politicising caste identities.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Caste grows
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Each caste group tries to grow by including neighbouring castes.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Better access
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Caste politics helped Dalits and OBCs gain better access to decision making.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Economic inequality
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Caste regulates access to resources, so it remains a source of economic inequality.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Old hierarchy persists
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Upper castes are best off, Dalits and Adivasis worst off, and backward classes in between.

examples: Caste politics has helped people from Dalit and OBC castes to gain better access to decision making.
National Sample Survey data shows upper castes are best off economically while Dalits and Adivasis are worst off.

importantKeywords.definition: The process by which caste identities are brought into and shaped by politics.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Caste groups uniting to gain political power shows the politicisation of caste.

importantKeywords.definition: A ladder-like formation in which caste groups are placed from the highest to the lowest.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Economic status still follows the old caste hierarchy.

importantKeywords.definition: Unequal distribution of wealth and resources among people.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Caste remains an important source of economic inequality in India.

topics.quickSummary: The relation between caste and politics is two-way: politics politicises caste, with caste groups expanding and Dalits and OBCs gaining better access to decision making. Caste also remains a source of economic inequality, since it once regulated access to land and education. National Sample Survey evidence shows that economic status still follows the old caste hierarchy - upper castes best off, Dalits and Adivasis worst off - even though every caste now has some rich and poor members.
revisionNotes: Politics politicises caste; relation is two-way.
Caste politics helped Dalits and OBCs gain access.
Caste is a source of economic inequality.

topics.topic: Meaning and Components of a Political Party
topics.meaning: A political party is a group of people who come together to contest elections and hold power in the government, with leaders, active members and followers.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A political party is a group of people who come together to contest elections and hold power in the government. They agree on some policies and programmes for the society with a view to promote the collective good, and since there can be different views on what is good for all, parties try to persuade people why their policies are better than others, seeking to implement them by winning popular support through elections. Thus parties reflect fundamental political divisions in a society and are about a part of the society, which involves partisanship; a party is known by which part it stands for, which policies it supports and whose interests it upholds. A political party has three components: the leaders, the active members and the followers. For most ordinary citizens democracy is equal to political parties, and even less educated citizens in remote parts of the country may know something about political parties, though this visibility does not mean popularity, as people tend to be critical of parties and blame them for the ills of democracy.
topics.easyExplanation: A political party is a group that contests elections and runs the government on the basis of agreed policies, and it has three parts - leaders, active members and followers.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Definition
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A political party is a group of people who come together to contest elections and hold power.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Members agree on policies and programmes to promote the collective good.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Persuasion through elections
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Parties persuade people that their policies are best and seek power through elections.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Partisanship
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A party stands for a part of society, so it involves partisanship.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Three components
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Every party has leaders, active members and followers.

examples: A political party contests elections to hold power and implement its agreed policies.
Every political party has three components - the leaders, the active members and the followers.

importantKeywords.simpleExample: A group agreeing on common policies and contesting elections is a political party.

For ordinary citizens, democracy is often equal to political parties.

topics.meaning: Political parties perform functions like contesting elections, forming policies, making laws, running governments, forming opposition, shaping opinion and providing access to welfare schemes.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Political parties fill political offices and exercise political power by performing a series of functions. First, parties contest elections, putting up candidates who are chosen by members in some countries like the USA and by top leaders in others like India. Second, parties put forward different policies and programmes, and the voters choose from them, with a party reducing a vast multitude of opinions into a few basic positions it supports. Third, parties play a decisive role in making laws, since most legislators belong to a party and follow the party leadership. Fourth, parties form and run governments, recruiting and training leaders and making them ministers. Fifth, those who lose elections play the role of opposition by criticising the government for its failures and mobilising opposition. Sixth, parties shape public opinion by raising and highlighting issues, with lakhs of members and activists, and many pressure groups are extensions of political parties. Seventh, parties provide people access to government machinery and welfare schemes, since it is easier for an ordinary citizen to approach a local party leader than a government officer.
topics.easyExplanation: Parties contest elections, make policies and laws, run governments, form the opposition, shape public opinion and help people access welfare schemes.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Contest elections
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Parties contest elections by putting up candidates chosen by members or leaders.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Policies and law-making
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Parties put forward policies and, as most legislators belong to a party, play a decisive role in making laws.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Form governments
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Parties form and run governments and recruit and train leaders as ministers.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Opposition and opinion
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Losing parties form the opposition and parties shape public opinion by raising issues.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Access to schemes
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Parties give ordinary citizens access to government machinery and welfare schemes.

examples: Parties contest elections and, in India, top party leaders choose candidates for contesting.
Losing parties form the opposition by criticising the government and mobilising opposition to it.

importantKeywords.definition: The political party that runs the government.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: The party that wins a majority becomes the ruling party.

importantKeywords.definition: Parties that lose elections and criticise the government and mobilise against it.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: The opposition questions the ruling party's failures in Parliament.

importantKeywords.definition: The shared views of people on issues, which parties help shape.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Parties shape public opinion by raising and highlighting issues.

topics.quickSummary: Political parties perform several functions: they contest elections; put forward policies and programmes for voters to choose; play a decisive role in making laws; form and run governments by recruiting and training leaders; form the opposition when they lose; shape public opinion by raising issues; and provide ordinary people access to government machinery and welfare schemes. Through these functions, parties fill political offices and exercise political power.
revisionNotes: Parties contest elections and form governments.
Decisive role in making laws and policies.
Losers form the opposition.
Shape public opinion and give access to welfare schemes.

topics.meaning: Modern democracies cannot exist without political parties because they make government accountable, gather views and run the country in a responsible way.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: We need political parties because they perform all the functions listed above, but we can understand their necessity more deeply by imagining a situation without parties. If there were no parties, every candidate in elections would be independent, so no one could make promises to the people about any major policy changes; a government might be formed, but its utility would remain uncertain. Elected representatives would only be accountable to their constituency for local work, but no one would be responsible for how the whole country is run. This can be seen in non-party based panchayat elections in many states, where even though parties do not formally contest, the village splits into factions, each putting up a panel of candidates - exactly what a party does. The rise of political parties is directly linked to the emergence of representative democracies, because large societies need representative democracy, and as societies became large and complex they needed agencies to gather different views, present them to government, and to support or restrain it. Parties fulfil these needs, so we can say that parties are a necessary condition for democracy.
topics.easyExplanation: Without parties, every candidate would be independent and no one would be responsible for running the whole country, which is why parties are necessary for democracy.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Imagine no parties
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Without parties every candidate would be independent.

stepByStepExplanation.title: No major promises
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Independent candidates could not promise major policy changes to the people.

stepByStepExplanation.title: No accountability for the country
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Representatives would answer only to their constituency, not for how the whole country is run.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Factions still form
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Even non-party panchayat elections split villages into factions putting up panels - like parties.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Linked to democracy
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Parties arose with representative democracy and are a necessary condition for it.

examples: In non-party panchayat elections, villages still split into factions, each putting up a panel of candidates, just like parties.
Without parties, no one would be responsible for how the whole country is run, only for local constituency work.

importantKeywords.definition: A democracy in which people rule through their elected representatives.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Large societies need representative democracy, which gave rise to parties.

importantKeywords.definition: Being answerable to the people for one's actions and decisions.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Parties ensure accountability for how the whole country is run.

importantKeywords.definition: A group within a larger body that takes a particular side, similar to a party.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Even non-party elections split villages into factions.

topics.quickSummary: Political parties are necessary because, without them, every candidate would be independent, unable to promise major policy changes, and no one would be responsible for running the whole country. Even non-party panchayat elections show villages splitting into faction-based panels, just like parties. Parties arose with representative democracy to gather views, present them to government, and support or restrain it, making them a necessary condition for democracy.
revisionNotes: Without parties, candidates would be independent.
No one accountable for running the whole country.
Non-party elections still form factions.
Parties are a necessary condition for democracy.

topics.topic: Party Systems: One-Party, Two-Party and Multi-Party
topics.meaning: Party systems describe how many effective parties contest for power - one-party, two-party or multi-party systems - and each evolves with a country's circumstances.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Although any group of citizens is free to form a party, only a handful are effectively in the race to win elections, so the question is how many major parties are good for a democracy. In a one-party system, only one party is allowed to control and run the government, as the Communist Party in China; this is not a good or democratic option because any democratic system must allow at least two parties to compete fairly. In a two-party system, power usually changes between two main parties, and although several others may exist, only the two main parties have a serious chance of winning a majority - the USA and the UK are examples. In a multi-party system, several parties compete and more than two have a reasonable chance of coming to power either on their own or in alliance, as in India; when several parties join hands to contest elections it is called an alliance or front, such as the National Democratic Alliance, the United Progressive Alliance and the Left Front in 2004. A party system is not chosen but evolves over a long time depending on the nature of society, its social and regional divisions and its history, and India's multi-party system reflects its great social and geographical diversity.
topics.easyExplanation: Party systems can be one-party (like China), two-party (like the USA and UK), or multi-party (like India), and each evolves from a country's society and history.

stepByStepExplanation.title: How many parties
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Only a handful of parties effectively contest, raising the question of how many are good.

stepByStepExplanation.title: One-party system
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Only one party is allowed to rule, as in China; this is not democratic.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Two-party system
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Power changes between two main parties, as in the USA and the UK.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Multi-party system
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Several parties can come to power on their own or in alliance, as in India.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Evolves over time
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A party system evolves with a country's society, divisions and history.

examples: The USA and the UK are examples of a two-party system where power changes between two main parties.
India has a multi-party system with alliances like the NDA, the UPA and the Left Front in the 2004 elections.

importantKeywords.definition: A system in which only one party is allowed to control and run the government.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: China, where only the Communist Party can rule, has a one-party system.

importantKeywords.definition: A system in which power usually changes between two main parties.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: The USA has a two-party system.

importantKeywords.definition: A system in which several parties compete and may come to power alone or in alliance.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: India has a multi-party system with several alliances.

topics.quickSummary: Party systems describe how many effective parties contest for power. A one-party system allows only one party to rule (China) and is undemocratic. A two-party system sees power change between two main parties (USA, UK). A multi-party system has several parties that can win alone or in alliances (India, with the NDA, UPA and Left Front). A party system is not chosen but evolves with a country's society, divisions and history.
revisionNotes: One-party: only one party rules (China) - undemocratic.
Two-party: power between two parties (USA, UK).
Multi-party: several parties and alliances (India).
Party systems evolve with society and history.

topics.meaning: National parties are country-wide recognised parties that have units in several states and follow the same policies decided at the national level.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Democracies that follow a federal system tend to have parties present in only one federal unit and parties present in several or all units; the latter are called national parties, which have units in various states but largely follow the same policies decided at the national level. Every party must register with the Election Commission, which treats all parties equally but offers special facilities such as a unique election symbol to large and established parties called recognised political parties. A party that secures at least six per cent of the total votes in an election to the Legislative Assembly of a state and wins at least two seats is recognised as a state party, while a party that secures at least six per cent of total votes in Lok Sabha or assembly elections in four states and wins at least four seats in the Lok Sabha is recognised as a national party. According to a 2023 notification of the Election Commission, there were six recognised national parties, including the Indian National Congress (Congress), the oldest party founded in 1885, which sought to build a modern secular democratic republic; the Bharatiya Janata Party founded in 1980 emphasising cultural nationalism; the Bahujan Samaj Party formed in 1984 under Kanshi Ram representing the bahujan samaj; the CPI(M); and others.
topics.easyExplanation: National parties are recognised country-wide parties with units in many states, recognised by the Election Commission based on vote share and seats, and include the Congress, BJP and BSP.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Two kinds of parties
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Federal democracies have parties in one unit and parties present in many units.

stepByStepExplanation.title: National parties
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Parties with units in several states following common national policies are national parties.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Registration
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Every party registers with the Election Commission, which gives recognised parties a unique symbol.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Recognition criteria
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A national party needs 6% votes and four Lok Sabha seats across four states.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Examples
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Recognised national parties include the Congress, BJP, BSP and CPI(M).

examples: The Indian National Congress, founded in 1885, is one of the oldest national parties and sought to build a secular democratic republic.
The Bharatiya Janata Party, founded in 1980, is a national party emphasising cultural nationalism.

importantKeywords.definition: A country-wide party with units in several states that follows common policies decided at the national level.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: The Congress and BJP are national parties.

importantKeywords.definition: A party given special facilities like a unique symbol by the Election Commission.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A recognised party gets a reserved election symbol.

importantKeywords.definition: A unique symbol given to a recognised party that only its official candidates may use.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Each recognised party has its own election symbol on the ballot.

topics.quickSummary: National parties are country-wide recognised parties with units in several states that follow common national policies. The Election Commission recognises a national party if it secures at least six per cent of votes in four states and wins at least four Lok Sabha seats, giving it a unique symbol. As per the 2023 notification, there were six recognised national parties, including the Congress (1885), BJP (1980), BSP (1984) and CPI(M).
revisionNotes: National parties have units in several states.
Need 6% votes in four states and four Lok Sabha seats.
Recognised parties get a unique symbol.
Six national parties as per 2023 notification.

topics.meaning: State parties, commonly called regional parties, are recognised parties that have succeeded mainly in one or a few states.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Other than the recognised national parties, most major parties of the country are classified by the Election Commission as state parties, commonly referred to as regional parties; yet these parties need not be regional in their ideology or outlook. Some of these are all-India parties that happen to have succeeded only in some states, such as the Samajwadi Party and the Rashtriya Janata Dal, which have national-level organisations with units in several states, while some others like the Biju Janata Dal, the Sikkim Democratic Front and the Telangana Rashtra Samithi are conscious about their state identity. Over the last three decades, the number and strength of these parties has expanded, making the Parliament politically more diverse, since no single national party was able on its own to secure a majority in the Lok Sabha until 2014, so national parties had to form alliances with state parties. Since 1996, nearly every state party has had an opportunity to be part of one or another national-level coalition government, which has contributed to the strengthening of federalism and democracy in the country.
topics.easyExplanation: State or regional parties are recognised parties that have succeeded mainly in one or a few states, and their growth has made Parliament more diverse and strengthened federalism.

stepByStepExplanation.title: What they are
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Most major parties besides national parties are classified as state or regional parties.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Not always regional
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: State parties need not be regional in ideology; some are all-India parties successful only in some states.

stepByStepExplanation.title: State identity
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Some, like the Biju Janata Dal and Sikkim Democratic Front, are conscious of their state identity.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Growing strength
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Over three decades their number and strength grew, making Parliament more diverse.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Strengthening federalism
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Since 1996 state parties joined national coalitions, strengthening federalism and democracy.

examples: The Samajwadi Party and Rashtriya Janata Dal are state parties with organisations in several states.
The Biju Janata Dal, Sikkim Democratic Front and Telangana Rashtra Samithi are state parties conscious of their state identity.

importantKeywords.definition: A recognised party that has succeeded mainly in one or a few states.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: The Biju Janata Dal is a state party in Odisha.

importantKeywords.definition: Another common name for a state party, recognised within particular states.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Regional parties often focus on the interests of their state.

importantKeywords.definition: A government formed by several parties coming together, often including state parties.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: National parties form coalition governments with state parties to win a majority.

topics.quickSummary: State parties, commonly called regional parties, are recognised parties that have succeeded mainly in one or a few states, though they need not be regional in ideology. They include all-India parties successful only in some states, like the Samajwadi Party, and state-identity parties like the Biju Janata Dal. Their growing number and strength has made Parliament more diverse and, since 1996, their participation in national coalitions has strengthened federalism and democracy.
revisionNotes: State parties succeed mainly in one or a few states.
Need not be regional in ideology.
Examples: Samajwadi Party, Biju Janata Dal, TRS.
Joined national coalitions, strengthening federalism.

topics.meaning: Political parties face four major challenges - lack of internal democracy, dynastic succession, the role of money and muscle power, and failure to offer meaningful choice.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Political parties face four problem areas. The first challenge is the lack of internal democracy within parties, as there is a tendency to concentrate power in one or a few leaders at the top; parties often do not keep membership registers, hold organisational meetings or conduct internal elections, so ordinary members lack information and influence, and personal loyalty to the leader becomes more important than loyalty to principles. The second challenge is dynastic succession, related to the first, since most parties do not practice open and transparent procedures, top positions are often controlled by members of one family, which is unfair and bad for democracy. The third challenge is the growing role of money and muscle power, especially during elections, as parties focused on winning nominate candidates who can raise lots of money, and rich people and companies who give funds influence party policies, while in some cases parties even support criminals who can win. The fourth challenge is that very often parties do not offer a meaningful choice to voters because the ideological differences among parties have declined - for example, the difference between the Labour Party and the Conservative Party in Britain is very little.
topics.easyExplanation: Parties face four challenges - they lack internal democracy, are controlled by families, are dominated by money and muscle power, and often fail to offer voters a real choice.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Lack of internal democracy
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Power is concentrated in top leaders, with no registers, meetings or internal elections.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Dynastic succession
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Top positions are often controlled by one family, which is unfair and bad for democracy.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Money and muscle power
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Parties nominate candidates who can raise money, and funders and criminals gain influence.

stepByStepExplanation.title: No meaningful choice
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Declining ideological differences mean parties often fail to offer voters a real choice.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Result
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: These challenges weaken parties as effective instruments of democracy.

examples: In many parties the top positions are always controlled by members of one family, showing dynastic succession.
The small difference between the Labour Party and the Conservative Party in Britain shows parties failing to offer meaningful choice.

importantKeywords.definition: Democratic functioning within a party, with registers, meetings and internal elections.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A party holding internal elections for its posts shows internal democracy.

importantKeywords.definition: The control of top party positions by members of one family.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: When party leadership passes within one family, it is dynastic succession.

importantKeywords.definition: The use of force or criminal influence to win elections.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Supporting criminals who can win elections shows the role of muscle power.

topics.quickSummary: Political parties face four challenges. The lack of internal democracy concentrates power in top leaders with no registers, meetings or internal elections. Dynastic succession lets one family control top posts. The growing role of money and muscle power lets funders and criminals influence parties. Finally, declining ideological differences mean parties often fail to offer voters a meaningful choice, as seen between Britain's Labour and Conservative parties.
revisionNotes: Lack of internal democracy.
Dynastic succession by one family.
Money and muscle power in elections.
Parties fail to offer meaningful choice.

topics.meaning: Political parties can be reformed through laws like the anti-defection law and affidavits, and suggestions like internal democracy, women's quotas and state funding.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: To face these challenges, political parties need to be reformed, but the difficulty is that the final decision is made by leaders who represent parties. Several recent efforts and suggestions have been made in India. The Constitution was amended to prevent elected MLAs and MPs from changing parties, so that any legislator who defects loses the seat - this anti-defection law brought defection down but made dissent more difficult. The Supreme Court passed an order making it mandatory for every candidate to file an affidavit giving details of property and criminal cases pending against him, which made information available to the public, though there is no system to check if it is true. The Election Commission passed an order making it necessary for parties to hold organisational elections and file income tax returns, but this is often a mere formality. Further suggestions include a law to regulate the internal affairs of parties, making it mandatory to give about one-third of tickets to women, and state funding of elections. However, these have not all been accepted, and over-regulation can be counter-productive; parties can also be reformed when people put pressure through petitions, publicity and agitations, and when ordinary citizens join parties.
topics.easyExplanation: Reforms include the anti-defection law, affidavits about candidates' wealth and crimes, and suggestions like internal democracy, one-third tickets for women, and state funding of elections.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Anti-defection law
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A legislator who changes parties loses the seat, reducing defection but limiting dissent.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Affidavits
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The Supreme Court made candidates file affidavits on property and pending criminal cases.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Election Commission order
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Parties must hold organisational elections and file income tax returns, though often as a formality.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Suggestions
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Suggestions include internal-democracy laws, one-third tickets for women, and state funding of elections.

stepByStepExplanation.title: People's role
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Parties can also be reformed by public pressure and by citizens joining them.

examples: The anti-defection law makes an MLA or MP who changes parties lose his seat.
It is suggested that parties be made to give about one-third of their tickets to women candidates.

importantKeywords.definition: Changing party allegiance from the party on which a person got elected to a different party.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: An MLA leaving his party to join another to become a minister commits defection.

importantKeywords.definition: A signed document submitted to an officer in which a person makes a sworn statement of personal information.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Candidates file an affidavit declaring their property and criminal cases.

importantKeywords.definition: Government support, in cash or kind, to parties for their election expenses.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Providing parties petrol or money based on past votes is state funding of elections.

topics.quickSummary: Parties can be reformed through laws and suggestions. The anti-defection law makes defecting legislators lose their seats; the Supreme Court requires candidates to file affidavits on property and criminal cases; and the Election Commission requires organisational elections and tax returns. Suggestions include regulating internal party affairs, giving one-third of tickets to women, and state funding of elections. Since leaders decide, public pressure and citizens joining parties also help, but over-regulation can be counter-productive.
revisionNotes: Anti-defection law: defectors lose their seats.
Affidavits on property and criminal cases.
Suggestions: internal democracy, women's quota, state funding.
Public pressure and participation also reform parties.

topics.meaning: Assessing democracy's outcomes means examining what we can reasonably expect from democracy, recognising it is just a form of government that creates conditions for achieving goals.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Most people support democracy as a better form of government than dictatorship, because it promotes equality among citizens, enhances the dignity of the individual, improves the quality of decision-making, provides a method to resolve conflicts, and allows room to correct mistakes. Yet not many are satisfied with democracy in practice, so we face a dilemma: democracy is seen to be good in principle but felt to be not so good in practice. Over a hundred countries today practice some kind of democratic politics with formal constitutions, elections, parties and rights, but these democracies differ greatly in their social situations, economic achievements and cultures. Our fascination with democracy can push us into expecting that it can address all socio-economic and political problems, and when some expectations are not met we start blaming the idea of democracy. The first step toward thinking carefully is to recognise that democracy is just a form of government that can only create conditions for achieving something - the citizens have to take advantage of those conditions and achieve those goals.
topics.easyExplanation: Democracy is good in principle - it promotes equality, dignity and better decisions - but it is only a form of government that creates conditions, and citizens must use those conditions to achieve goals.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Why democracy is better
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Democracy promotes equality, dignity, better decisions, conflict resolution and correcting mistakes.

stepByStepExplanation.title: The dilemma
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Democracy is seen as good in principle but felt to be not so good in practice.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Many democracies differ
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Over a hundred democracies differ greatly in social, economic and cultural terms.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Avoid over-expectation
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Expecting democracy to solve all problems leads to blaming the idea of democracy.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Just a form of government
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Democracy creates conditions; citizens must use them to achieve goals.

examples: Democracy promotes equality among citizens and enhances the dignity of the individual.
Democracy allows room to correct mistakes, unlike non-democratic forms of government.

importantKeywords.definition: The result or product that we can reasonably expect from democracy.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A legitimate government is an outcome of democracy.

importantKeywords.definition: The respect and worth that democracy aims to give every individual.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Democracy enhances the dignity of the individual.

importantKeywords.definition: Providing a peaceful method to settle disagreements among groups.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Democracy provides a method of conflict resolution.

topics.quickSummary: Democracy is supported as better than dictatorship because it promotes equality, dignity, better decision-making, conflict resolution and the ability to correct mistakes. Yet people are often dissatisfied with democracy in practice, creating a dilemma. Since over a hundred democracies differ widely, and over-expectation leads to blaming democracy, the key is to recognise that democracy is just a form of government that creates conditions, which citizens must use to achieve their goals.
revisionNotes: Democracy promotes equality, dignity and better decisions.
Good in principle but often felt weak in practice.
Democracy only creates conditions, not guarantees.
Citizens must use the conditions to achieve goals.

topics.meaning: Democracy produces a government that is accountable to citizens, responsive to their needs, and legitimate because it is the people's own government.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: In a democracy we are most concerned that people have the right to choose their rulers and have control over them, so the most basic outcome of democracy should be a government that is accountable to the citizens and responsive to their needs and expectations. Some people think democracy produces less effective government because non-democratic rulers do not have to bother about deliberation or public opinion and can decide quickly, while democracy is based on deliberation and negotiation so some delay occurs. But thinking in terms of costs, a fast decision not accepted by the people may face problems, while a democratic decision that follows procedures may be more acceptable and effective, so the cost of time is worth it. Democracy also ensures decision-making is based on norms and procedures, so a citizen has the right to examine the process of decision-making, which is called transparency. The actual performance of democracies is mixed on accountability, openness and corruption, but in one respect democratic government is certainly better than its alternatives: it is a legitimate government - it may be slow, less efficient or not always clean, but it is the people's own government, which is why there is overwhelming support for democracy all over the world.
topics.easyExplanation: Democracy gives an accountable, responsive and transparent government, and although it may be slow, it is legitimate because it is the people's own government.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Control over rulers
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Democracy gives people the right to choose and control their rulers.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Accountable and responsive
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The basic outcome is a government accountable to citizens and responsive to their needs.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Cost of time
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Democracy may be slower, but its decisions are more acceptable and effective.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Transparency
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Citizens can examine the process of decision-making, which is transparency.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Legitimate government
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Democracy is legitimate because it is the people's own government, drawing overwhelming support.

examples: A citizen in a democracy has the right to examine whether a decision was taken through correct procedures, which is transparency.
Democratic governments hold regular free elections and allow open public debate, making them accountable.

importantKeywords.definition: A government that is answerable to the citizens for its actions.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A government that must explain its decisions to the people is accountable.

importantKeywords.definition: The right and means of a citizen to examine the process of decision-making.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Checking whether a decision followed correct procedures shows transparency.

importantKeywords.definition: A government that is the people's own, chosen and accepted by them.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A democratically elected government is a legitimate government.

topics.quickSummary: The basic outcome of democracy is a government accountable to citizens and responsive to their needs, where people choose and control their rulers. Although democracy may be slower because of deliberation, its decisions are more acceptable and effective, and it ensures transparency by letting citizens examine decision-making. Its record on corruption is mixed, but democracy is certainly legitimate - it is the people's own government, which is why it enjoys overwhelming worldwide support.
revisionNotes: People can choose and control their rulers.
Government is accountable and responsive.
Transparency lets citizens examine decisions.
Democracy is legitimate - the people's own government.

topics.meaning: Democracy is not a guarantee of economic development, but its record is not significantly behind that of dictatorships, especially among poor countries.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: If democracies are expected to produce good governments, it is fair to expect them to produce development too, but evidence shows that in practice many democracies did not fully fulfil this expectation. If we consider all democracies and dictatorships for the fifty years between 1950 and 2000, dictatorships have a slightly higher rate of economic growth. However, this alone cannot be a reason to reject democracy, because economic development depends on several factors such as a country's population size, global situation, cooperation from other countries and economic priorities. Importantly, the difference in the rates of economic development between less developed countries with dictatorships and democracies is negligible - when we compare records only in poor countries, there is virtually no difference. Overall, we cannot say democracy is a guarantee of economic development, but we can expect democracy not to lag behind dictatorships in this respect. Since there is such a small difference, and democracy has several other positive outcomes, it is better to prefer democracy.
topics.easyExplanation: Dictatorships had slightly higher growth between 1950 and 2000, but among poor countries there is almost no difference, so democracy does not lag behind and is still preferable.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Expectation of development
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: It is fair to expect democracies to produce development as well as good government.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Slightly higher dictatorship growth
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: From 1950-2000, dictatorships had a slightly higher rate of economic growth overall.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Many factors
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Development depends on population, global situation, cooperation and economic priorities.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Negligible difference for poor countries
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Among poor countries the difference between democracies and dictatorships is virtually nil.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Prefer democracy
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Since the difference is small and democracy has other positive outcomes, it is preferable.

examples: Between 1950 and 2000, dictatorships had a slightly higher overall economic growth rate than democracies.
Among poor countries, the difference in growth rates between democracies and dictatorships is negligible.

importantKeywords.definition: The growth in a country's wealth and improvement in living standards.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Democracy is not a guarantee of economic development.

importantKeywords.definition: The increase in the production of goods and services in a country.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Dictatorships had a slightly higher economic growth rate from 1950 to 2000.

importantKeywords.definition: Poorer countries with lower levels of economic development.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Among less developed countries, democracy and dictatorship show negligible growth differences.

topics.quickSummary: Democracy is not a guarantee of economic development. Between 1950 and 2000, dictatorships had a slightly higher growth rate overall, but development depends on many factors like population and global situation. Crucially, among poor countries the difference between democracies and dictatorships is negligible. Since democracy does not significantly lag behind and has several other positive outcomes, it remains the preferable form of government.
revisionNotes: Democracy is not a guarantee of development.
1950-2000: dictatorships had slightly higher growth.
Among poor countries, the difference is negligible.
Democracy preferable due to other positive outcomes.

topics.meaning: Democracies are based on political equality but do not appear very successful in reducing economic inequalities and poverty.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Perhaps more than development, it is reasonable to expect democracies to reduce economic disparities, because democracies are based on political equality where all individuals have equal weight in electing representatives. But parallel to bringing individuals into the political arena on an equal footing, we find growing economic inequalities - a small number of ultra-rich enjoy a highly disproportionate share of wealth and income, and their share has been increasing, while those at the bottom have very little and their incomes have been declining, sometimes making it hard to meet basic needs like food, clothing, housing, education and health. In actual life, democracies do not appear very successful in reducing economic inequalities. The poor constitute a large proportion of voters and no party likes to lose their votes, yet democratically elected governments do not appear keen to address poverty. The situation is much worse in some other countries - in Bangladesh more than half the population lives in poverty, and people in several poor countries depend on rich countries even for food supplies. Tables also show that within democracies like South Africa and Brazil, the top 20 per cent take away more than 60 per cent of national income, while countries like Denmark and Hungary are much more equal.
topics.easyExplanation: Democracies give everyone equal political rights but have not been very successful in reducing the growing gap between rich and poor or in removing poverty.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Political equality
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Democracies are based on political equality where every vote counts equally.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Growing economic inequality
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Alongside political equality, economic inequalities grow, with the ultra-rich getting a bigger share.

stepByStepExplanation.title: The poor struggle
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Those at the bottom have declining incomes and struggle to meet basic needs.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Limited success
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Democracies do not appear keen or successful in addressing poverty despite poor voters.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Wide disparities
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: In democracies like South Africa and Brazil, the top 20% take over 60% of national income.

examples: In democratic countries like South Africa and Brazil, the top 20 per cent take away more than 60 per cent of national income.
In Bangladesh, more than half of the population lives in poverty despite being a democracy.

importantKeywords.definition: The principle that all individuals have equal weight in electing representatives.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: One person, one vote reflects political equality.

importantKeywords.definition: The unequal distribution of wealth and income among people.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A few ultra-rich holding most of the wealth shows economic inequality.

importantKeywords.definition: The condition of being unable to meet basic needs like food, clothing and housing.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: More than half of Bangladesh's population lives in poverty.

topics.quickSummary: Democracies are based on political equality, giving every vote equal weight, but they have not been very successful in reducing economic inequality and poverty. The ultra-rich take a growing and disproportionate share of income while the poor struggle with declining incomes. Despite the poor being a large share of voters, governments seem unkeen to address poverty. In democracies like South Africa and Brazil the top 20 per cent take over 60 per cent of national income.

topics.meaning: Democracy is best suited to accommodate social diversity and reduce conflict, provided the majority works with the minority and does not become permanent rule by one community.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: It is a fair expectation that democracy should produce a harmonious social life, and democracies usually develop procedures to conduct competition among groups, reducing the possibility of tensions becoming explosive or violent. We saw how Belgium successfully negotiated differences among ethnic populations, and the ability to handle social differences, divisions and conflicts is a definite plus point of democratic regimes, since non-democratic regimes often turn a blind eye to or suppress internal differences. No society can fully and permanently resolve conflicts among different groups, but it can learn to respect differences and evolve mechanisms to negotiate them. However, the example of Sri Lanka reminds us that a democracy must fulfil two conditions to achieve this outcome. First, it is necessary to understand that democracy is not simply rule by majority opinion - the majority always needs to work with the minority so that government represents the general view, since majority and minority opinions are not permanent. Second, rule by majority must not become rule by one majority community in terms of religion, race or language, because democracy remains democracy only as long as every citizen has a chance of being in the majority at some point.
topics.easyExplanation: Democracy is best at accommodating social diversity and reducing conflict, but only if the majority works with the minority and does not become permanent rule by one religious or linguistic community.

topics.topic: Dignity and Freedom of the Citizens
topics.meaning: Democracy is superior to other forms of government in promoting the dignity and freedom of individuals, including women and disadvantaged castes.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Democracy stands much superior to any other form of government in promoting the dignity and freedom of the individual, because every individual wants respect from fellow beings and the passion for respect and freedom is the basis of democracy. Democracies throughout the world have recognised this, at least in principle, in various degrees. Take the case of the dignity of women: most societies were historically male-dominated, and long struggles by women created sensitivity that respect and equal treatment of women are necessary ingredients of a democratic society; once this principle is recognised, it becomes easier for women to wage struggles against what is now unacceptable legally and morally. The same is true of caste inequalities - democracy in India has strengthened the claims of the disadvantaged and discriminated castes for equal status and equal opportunity, and although caste-based inequalities and atrocities still exist, they now lack moral and legal foundations. What is most distinctive about democracy is that its examination never gets over - as it passes one test it produces another, because as people get benefits they ask for more. The fact that people complain is itself a testimony to democracy's success, because it transforms people from the status of a subject into that of a citizen.
topics.easyExplanation: Democracy is the best form of government for promoting dignity and freedom, giving women and disadvantaged castes a legal and moral basis to demand equality, and people's complaints show democracy is succeeding.

topics.meaning: Democracy may take more time to decide because it follows procedures, but this makes its decisions more acceptable and effective.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A common question is whether democratic government is efficient and effective, since some people think democracy produces less effective government. It is true that non-democratic rulers do not have to bother about deliberation in assemblies or worry about majorities and public opinion, so they can be very quick and efficient in decision-making and implementation. Democracy, by contrast, is based on the idea of deliberation and negotiation, so some delay is bound to take place. But this does not necessarily make democratic government inefficient when we think in terms of costs. Imagine a government that takes decisions very fast but takes decisions that are not accepted by the people and therefore faces problems; in contrast, a democratic government takes more time to follow procedures before arriving at a decision, but because it has followed procedures its decisions may be both more acceptable to the people and more effective. So the cost of time that democracy pays is perhaps worth it. Democracy also ensures decision-making is based on norms and procedures and gives citizens the right to examine the process, which is transparency, a factor often missing from non-democratic governments.
topics.easyExplanation: Democracy is slower because it deliberates and follows procedures, but this makes its decisions more acceptable and effective, so the delay is worth it.

topics.topic: Democracy as the Rule of the People
topics.meaning: The most distinctive outcome of democracy is that it is the rule of the people, generating its own support and continually inviting people to make it better.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: There is one respect in which democratic government is certainly better than its alternatives: a democratic government is a legitimate government, because it may be slow, less efficient, not always responsive or clean, but it is the people's own government. That is why there is overwhelming support for the idea of democracy all over the world; as evidence from South Asia shows, this support exists in countries with democratic regimes as well as those without, with people wishing to be ruled by representatives elected by them and believing democracy is suitable for their country. Democracy's ability to generate its own support is itself an outcome that cannot be ignored. What is most distinctive about democracy is that its examination never gets over - as democracy passes one test it produces another, because as people get benefits they ask for more and want to make democracy even better. The fact that people complain about democracy is itself a testimony to its success, because it shows people have developed the awareness and ability to look critically at power-holders. A public expression of dissatisfaction with democracy shows the success of the democratic project, as it transforms people from the status of subjects into citizens who believe their vote makes a difference.
topics.easyExplanation: Democracy's best outcome is that it is the people's own government, which generates its own support, and even people's complaints show democracy is working by turning them into aware citizens.
